mission.title: Install and Configure Devspace on Kubernetes
mission.description: DevSpace is a powerful developer tool that automates deployment workflows and enables development directly inside Kubernetes. Installing DevSpace will streamline your cloud-native development process, allowing for faster iterations and easier collaboration within your team.

steps.title: Add the DevSpace Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official DevSpace Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add devspace https://charts.devspace.sh
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install DevSpace using Helm
steps.description:
Install DevSpace in a new namespace called 'devspace' with the following command:
```bash
helm install devspace devspace/devspace --version v6.3.20 --namespace devspace --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the DevSpace pods are running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace devspace
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To set resource limits for your DevSpace deployment, edit the `devspace.yaml` file and add the following under the `deployments` section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Enable hot reloading
steps.description:
To enable hot reloading, ensure your `devspace.yaml` file has the following configuration:
```yaml
dev:
  watch:
    enabled: true
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of DevSpace will have the DevSpace pods running in the 'devspace' namespace. You will also have a configured `devspace.yaml` file enabling resource limits and hot reloading for your development workflow.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add devspace https://charts.devspace.sh
helm repo update
helm install devspace devspace/devspace --version v6.3.20 --namespace devspace --create-namespace

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the DevSpace release from the Kubernetes cluster using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall devspace --namespace devspace
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with DevSpace:
```bash
kubectl delete crd devspaces.devspace.sh
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace devspace
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'devspace' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace devspace
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of your DevSpace release:
```bash
helm get values devspace --namespace devspace > devspace-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the DevSpace release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade devspace devspace/devspace --version v6.3.21 --namespace devspace
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the DevSpace pods are running the new version after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace devspace
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your DevSpace pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace devspace
``` 
Look for any error messages that might indicate the cause of the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with 'release not found'
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating 'release not found' during an upgrade, ensure that the release name and namespace are correct:
```bash
helm list --namespace devspace
``` 
If the release does not exist, you may need to install it instead.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are being killed due to Out Of Memory (OOM), consider increasing the memory limits in your `devspace.yaml` file:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "1Gi"
``` 
After updating, redeploy your application.

troubleshooting.title: Network issues preventing pod communication
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are unable to communicate with each other, check the network policies and service configurations:
```bash
kubectl get networkpolicies --namespace devspace
kubectl get services --namespace devspace
``` 
Ensure that the necessary ports are open and services are correctly defined.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
